title: CHAPTER 1
summary: Catherine Morland had a sallow skin without colour, dark lank hair, and strong features. She was fond of all boys’ plays, and greatly preferred cricket not merely to dolls. At the age of 14 she began to curl her hair and long for balls. At 15, appearances were improving, and she grew clean as she grew smart.

title: CHAPTER 2
summary: Catherine Morland’s heart was affectionate; her disposition cheerful and open, without conceit or affectation of any kind. Her father gave her only ten pounds bank-bill, and promised her more when she wanted it. Her mother knew so little of lords and baronets, that she entertained no notion of their mischievousness.

title: CHAPTER 3
summary: “I see what you think of me,” said he gravely. Catherine turned away her head, not knowing whether she might venture to laugh. “Shall I tell you what you ought to say?” “If you please.” Catherine coloured, and said, “I was not thinking of anything.’

title: CHAPTER 4
summary: Catherine Allen went to meet Mr. Tilney at the pump-room the next day, but he did not appear. Mrs. Thorpe, however, was a talker, over Mrs. Allen, in a family of children. She expatiated on the talents of her sons and the beauty of her daughters.

title: CHAPTER 5
summary: Catherine was not so much engaged at the theatre that evening, in returning the nods and smiles of Miss Thorpe, as to forget to look with an inquiring eye for Mr. Tilney in every box which her eye could reach. Catherine was never satisfied with the day unless she spent the chief of it by the side of Mrs. Thorpe.

title: CHAPTER 6
summary: Catherine and Isabella were friends for eight or nine days. They met by appointment. Catherine asked Isabella what she had been doing with herself. Isabella said she was reading Udolpho, and had made a list of horrid books for a friend. Catherine replied that she preferred light eyes, and a sallow better than any other.

title: CHAPTER 7
summary: Isabella Thorpe was delighted to meet her brother-in-law. Catherine was not so delighted, however, when she saw the carriage carrying James Morland and his friend. James and Isabella led the way; and so well-satisfied was the latter with her lot, so contentedly was she contentedly.

title: CHAPTER 8
summary: Isabella and James Morland were dancing with Isabella's sister, Catherine, and her brother, Mr. Tilney. Isabella wanted to see her brother again, but James would not let her go. James said: “My dear Isabella, how was it possible for me to get at you? I could not even see where you were. Do go and see for her”

title: CHAPTER 9
summary: Catherine's unhappiness from the events of the evening was as follows. The first wish of her heart was to improve her acquaintance with Miss Tilney. She made almost her first resolution, to seek her for that purpose, in the pump-room at noon. Catherine was in much perplexity, and was more than once on the. point of requesting from Mr. Thorpe a clearer insight into his real.opinion.

title: CHAPTER 10
summary: Catherine was at a party in Bath with her brother and his wife. The party was attended by the Thorpe family, attended by James Morland. Catherine was delighted to see her friend, Miss Tilney, who was also there. Catherine and her brother were engaged to Mr. Thorpe at the time.

title: CHAPTER 11
summary: Catherine Allen and her family were going to Clifton to visit Kingsweston and Blaize Castle. The weather was forecast to clear up, but it was too dirty for Mrs. Allen to accompany her husband. John Thorpe set off by himself, and Catherine had barely watched him down the street when her notice was claimed by the approach of two open carriages.

title: CHAPTER 12
summary: “Mrs. Allen,” said Catherine the next morning, “will there be any harm in my calling on Miss Tilney to-day? I shall not be easy till I have explained everything” “Go, by all means, my dear; only put on a white gown,’ said Mrs. Allen. “Miss Tilney always wears white” Catherine cheerfully complied.

title: CHAPTER 13
summary: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, and Saturday have now been passed in review before the reader. The events of each day, its hopes, fears, and pleasures, have been separately stated. The pangs of Sunday only now remain to be described, and close the week.

title: CHAPTER 14
summary: “The person, be it gentleman or lady, who has not pleasure in a good novel, must be intolerably stupid” “Henry,” said Miss Tilney, “you are very impertinent. Miss Morland, he is treating you exactly as he does his sister. He is forever finding fault with me, for some incorrectness of language, and now he is taking the same liberty with you.”

title: CHAPTER 15
summary: Isabella Morland was engaged to be married to James Morland. She wrote to her father-in-law asking for permission to marry him. Her parents agreed, and James wrote a letter of congratulations. Catherine's reaction to the news of James's visit to Fullerton. She regretted the necessity of its concealment.

title: CHAPTER 16
summary: Catherine’s expectations of pleasure from her visit in Milsom Street were so very high that disappointment was inevitable. Instead of finding herself improved in acquaintance with Miss Tilney, she seemed hardly so intimate with her as before. Henry asked Catherine if she thought her friend, Miss Thorpe, would have any objection to dancing. Catherine, without hesitation, replied that she was very sure MissThorpe did not mean to dance at all.

title: CHAPTER 17
summary: Catherine Tilney was invited to stay at Northanger Abbey by her father's friends, the Morlands. The Allens were also invited, and Catherine wrote to them to ask for permission to visit. Catherine was delighted to be able to spend time with the Allens and their daughter, Isabella.

title: CHAPTER 18
summary: Catherine protested her innocence of every thought of Mr. Thorpe’s being in love with her, and the consequent impossibility of her having ever intended to encourage him. “I certainly cannot return his affection, and as certainly never meant to encourage it” Isabella was silent. Captain Tilney wrote to Isabella. ‘I would not for all the world be the means of hurrying you into an engagement’

title: CHAPTER 19
summary: Isabella seemed an altered customizablecreature. When she saw her in public, admitting Captain Tilney’s attentions, and allowing him almost an equal share of her notice and smiles, the alteration became too positive. “I understand: she is in love with James, and flirts with Frederick.“ “Oh no, not flirts. A woman in love. with one man cannot flirt with another”

title: CHAPTER 20
summary: Mr. and Mrs. Allen were sorry to lose their young friend, whose good humour and cheerfulness had made her a valuable companion. Her happiness in going with Miss Tilney, however, prevented their wishing it otherwise. She was so fearful of not doing exactly what was right, and of not being able to preserve their good opinion, that she could almost have wished to return with him.

title: CHAPTER 21
summary: Catherine Morland arrived at her new home in London. She was at first impressed by the surroundings. A large chest stood in the corner of the room. It contained a mysterious cipher in silver. Catherine bent over it, but without being able to distinguish anything with certainty. She seized a second, a third, a fourth; each was equally empty.

title: CHAPTER 22
summary: Henry and Catherine Morland visited Woodston, the General's country home. Catherine was all impatience to see the house, and had scarcely any curiosity about the grounds. She was struck, however, by the grandeur of the abbey, as she saw it for the first time. The General was flattered by her looks.

title: CHAPTER 23
summary: Catherine Allen was taken to the abbey by the General, who showed her around. She was impressed, beyond her expectation, by the comforts and convenience of the rooms. She saw him in the evening while she worked with her friend, slowly pacing the drawing-room for an hour together in silent thoughtfulness.

title: CHAPTER 24
summary: The next day afforded no opportunity for the proposed examination of the mysterious apartments. The day was unmarked by anything to interest her imagination beyond the sight of a very elegant monument to the memory of Mrs. Tilney. Catherine had depended upon meeting features, hair, complexion, that should be the very image, if not of Henry's, of Eleanor’s.

title: CHAPTER 25
summary: The visions of romance were over. Catherine was completely awakened. Her folly, which now seemed even criminal, was all criminal, and he must despise her forever. She hated herself more than she could express. Among the English, Catherine believed, there was a general though unequal mixture of good and bad. Henry’s astonishing generosity and nobleness of conduct was of the greatest assistance to her. Her only dependence for information of any kind was on Isabella.

title: CHAPTER 26
summary: Catherine's two young friends were perfectly agreed in considering Isabella’s want ofsequence and fortune as likely to throw great difficulties in the way of her marrying their brother. Their persuasion that the General would, upon this ground alone, independent of the objection that might be raised against her character, oppose the connection, turned her with some alarm towards herself.

title: CHAPTER 27
summary: The next morning brought the following very unexpected letter from Isabella to Catherine. Isabella wrote: I have had my pen in my hand to begin a letter to you almost every day since you left Bath, but have always been prevented by some silly trifler or other. She wrote: “I see that she has designs on Captain Tilney, which have not succeeded” “Well, then, I must say that I do not like him at all,” said Catherine.

title: CHAPTER 28
summary: Catherine was in London for a week with Eleanor Morland and Henry Morland. The General had to go to London, leaving Catherine with Eleanor and the children. Catherine was in love with the people and the place, and loved them more every day. She was dreading leaving the one and not being equally loved by the other.

title: CHAPTER 29
summary: Catherine returned to Fullerton after an eleven-week absence. Her parents were shocked by the behaviour of General Tilney. Catherine's parents saw nothing in her looks and agitation but the natural consequence of mortified and fatigue of such a journey. They never once thought of her heart, which, for the parents of a young lady of seventeen, just returned from her first.excursion from home, was odd enough!

title: CHAPTER 30
summary: Henry Tilney was a guest at the home of Catherine Morland and her family. Catherine meanwhile was anxious, agitated, happy, feverish and brightened by his presence. Henry Morland met Isabella Morland at the abbey and asked her to accompany him on a walk with his father. The General was furious in his anger, and they parted in dreadful disagreement.

title: CHAPTER 31
summary: Mr. and Mrs. Morland’s surprise on being applied to by Mr. Tilney for their consent to his marrying their daughter was, for a few minutes, it having never entered their heads to suspect an evil. Northanger Abbey was written in 1797–98 under a different title. The manuscript was revised around 1803 and sold to a London publisher. The Signet Classic text is based on the first edition, published by John Murray in 1818.
